title: The ASI Stack
subtitle: A Systems Architecture for Governed, Efficient, Self-Improving AI
author: Corben Sorenson

parts.title: Part I - Foundations, Alignment, and Governance
parts.part_job: Establish the first-principles frame: ASI as a governed stack, the efficiency hypothesis, the failure model, the evidence discipline, and the constitutional/governance substrate that bounds every later layer.

chapters.title: ASI Is a Stack, Not a Model
chapters.problem: The book needs a single architecture frame for advanced AI systems that must plan, remember, verify, act, route work, compress representations, and improve under governance.
chapters.insufficient: A larger model, a prompt wrapper, or an agent loop does not by itself define authority boundaries, memory discipline, evidence ledgers, tool permissions, or safe replacement rules.
chapters.core_claim: Efficient ASI should be modeled as a governed stack of cooperating layers rather than as one undifferentiated model.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Frames advanced AI as intent-to-execution machinery that turns human intent into command contracts, durable artifacts, routing, verification, runtime execution, feedback, residuals, tools, benchmarks, and regressions.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Architecture proposal and systems framework only; does not prove a completed deployment, benchmark result, or end-to-end ASI stack in this repository.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports the whole-stack framing with local, stateful intelligence split across memory, verification, planning, routing, security, consolidation, and interface layers rather than a stateless model.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Hardware, cost, context-length, and benchmark claims are source-reported and not locally reproduced.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports separating generation from trusted output through context acquisition, claim decomposition, verification gates, uncertainty routing, and constitutional governance.
claim_source_mappings.limits: No local Aletheia implementation, live-oracle run, adversarial result, or proof artifact is present here.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports the execution/labor layer by treating AI work as typed jobs, deterministic control planes, evidence, isolation, audit, replay, delivery, and residual feedback.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Useful as an execution-OS design source, not proof that security or benchmark behavior has been reproduced in this repository.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports implementation-reference context for a compact orchestrator, specialist lanes, fail-closed control plane, ledgers, readiness gates, replay, and handoff.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Connector-readable source reports architecture state and artifacts, but this repository has not ingested or reproduced MoECOT code, logs, benchmark artifacts, or runtime claims.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports the governed-stack thesis by defining stable capability fields as replaceable machinery behind durable contracts, evidence registries, qualification, routing, authority, lifecycle, and recovery paths.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Does not establish production safety, global alignment, or independently validated reversibility for the whole stack.

mechanism: Define each layer by responsibility, interface, artifact, invariant, and failure mode.
Frame the raw LLM as a semantic-compression and generation component inside a larger governed cognitive system.
Treat the whole book as a reference architecture rather than a collection of standalone papers.
Use source queues and evidence states to keep future writing runs context-loaded and honest.
interfaces: Alignment and governance constrain every downstream layer.
Planning, memory, reasoning, execution, routing, compression, and evidence exchange typed artifacts.
Recursive improvement is a governed transition, not an ambient property of intelligence.
invariants: Reasoning ability never implies execution authority.
failure_modes: Evidence inflation before source notes or tests exist.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A stack map, layer-boundary table, source crosswalk, and claim ledger that make the architecture navigable before prose is complete.

proof_targets.target: A layer without external-action authority can only produce an action through an authorized handoff.
A handoff that exceeds the caller authority ceiling is rejected.

chapters.title: The Efficient ASI Hypothesis
chapters.problem: The architecture needs a non-scale-only theory for how capability can increase without using maximal cognition for every subtask.
chapters.insufficient: Parameter count alone does not explain routing, context selectivity, procedural memory, compression, runtime targeting, or verification effort.
chapters.core_claim: Capability improves when cognition is routed, compressed, reused, governed, and tested with visible residuals.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports efficient capability growth through routed work, workflow-to-tool compilation, durable artifacts, feedback, residuals, benchmarks, and regression coverage.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Does not provide reproduced route-cost measurements, deployment logs, or benchmark results in this repository.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports the ratchet loop: benchmark pressure, specialist attempts, logs, loop closure, residual escrow, diagnosis, arm/router improvement, regression preservation, and harder frontiers.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Conceptual architecture only here; no independent reproduction, benchmark run, prototype inspection, or Lean proof was performed from this source note.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports the smallest-adequate-structure idea with compact seeds, rule systems, memory/state, residual/error, verification, and governance/generation interfaces.
claim_source_mappings.limits: No local CGS benchmark, proof, or implementation validates compactness, safety, interpretability, or adequacy.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports artifact-compression economics through per-artifact transforms, residual bit cost, deterministic reconstruction, manifests, codec parameters, and checks.
claim_source_mappings.limits: No local compression benchmarks, implementation artifacts, or reproducible ratios are present.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports generate-verify-repair compression, contract-relative reconstruction, retained detail, residuals, repair streams, fallback, boundary costs, and proxy-rate honesty.
claim_source_mappings.limits: The repository has not implemented BBVCA Prototype A or reproduced compression ratios.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports contract-relative feasibility by tying resource demand to scope, clockspeed, fidelity, efficiency, compute, memory, thermodynamics, and communication bottlenecks.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Theoretical synthesis only here; no physical experiment, simulation benchmark, or independent literature audit was run.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports efficiency-by-architecture through local state, persistent memory substrates, routing by semantic intent/tier/critical path/capability, and paging of attention, experts, and memory.
claim_source_mappings.limits: SSD-first, hardware, and performance claims remain source-reported and unvalidated locally.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports roadmap-independent BeastBrain framing for local intelligence, hardware adaptation, SSD-native memory, planning, verification, and multimodal interaction as architecture directions.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Does not validate hardware adaptation, geometric verification, or distributed scaling; use mainly for variant deduplication and evergreen framing.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports spending verification and context effort by risk, uncertainty, ambiguity, and historical failure rather than treating all outputs as equally trusted.
claim_source_mappings.limits: No local live-oracle, adversarial, or implementation evidence proves the efficiency or quality of those routes.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports compact orchestration, specialist lanes, readiness gates, promotion blockers, residual tracking, ledgers, replay, and handoff as implementation-reference context for efficient routing.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Source-reported runtime and benchmark artifacts have not been ingested or reproduced here.

mechanism: Use minimum viable intelligence for subtasks.
Turn repeated cognition into durable artifacts, tools, or procedural memory.
Model efficiency through the seed/router/search/generator/verifier/residual loop plus memory and governance.
Expose residual burden when routing or compression is incomplete.
interfaces: Evidence decides whether efficiency claims survived quality and cost tests.
invariants: Efficiency claims include quality and cost.
failure_modes: Benchmark gains that increase hidden residual burden.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A costed route ledger with quality predicates, residual accounting, and fallback criteria.

proof_targets.target: A route is minimum viable only when no lower-cost authorized route satisfies the required quality predicate.
A routed or compressed result with open obligations cannot be promoted without a residual record.

chapters.title: System Boundaries and Authority
chapters.problem: The stack needs a formal vocabulary for boundaries, authority ceilings, permissions, and handoffs before any layer can be made safe or testable.
chapters.insufficient: Informal ownership boundaries let planning authority, memory access, tool use, and deployment authority blur into each other.
chapters.core_claim: Authority should be modeled as a typed, bounded capability attached to layers, fields, tools, artifacts, and principals.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports authority boundaries through structured command fields, constraints, verification, failure behavior, artifact graphs, runtime adapters, and ledgers that separate intent, work, evidence, and execution.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Does not prove a deployed VIEA authority system or runtime enforcement in this repository.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports typed bounded authority with stable fields, contracts, qualifications, grants, route validation, lifecycle events, evaluator policy, and recovery paths.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Does not establish production safety, global alignment, or independently validated route/evaluator behavior.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports effect control through typed job lifecycles, contract locks, source allow-listing, blind secret handles, Digital SCIFs, audit logs, replay, controlled runtime adapters, and approvals.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Security and benchmark claims require separate artifacts; this mapping is architectural, not a reproduced enforcement test.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports authority-handle boundaries by keeping credential bytes outside model context, using opaque handles, policy-mediated secret injection, isolated compartments, audit, and zeroization.
claim_source_mappings.limits: No Ladon implementation, kernel test, side-channel validation, or security audit exists in this repository.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports proposal/execution separation through a small deterministic kernel, capability boundaries for nondeterministic effects, semantic patches, provenance hashes, replay logs, obligations, and protocol seals.
claim_source_mappings.limits: No GenesisCode prototype, replay checker, proof module, benchmark result, or security audit is present here.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports fail-closed runtime authority through compact orchestration, specialist lanes, control-plane ledgers, readiness gates, promotion blockers, replay, and handoff.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Runtime and benchmark claims remain implementation-reference context until artifacts are imported, inspected, or reproduced.

mechanism: Define principals, authorities, ceilings, grants, revocations, and handoff contracts.
Separate knowledge access from action authority.
Represent missing authority as a detectable failure rather than implicit permission.
invariants: Read permission is not write permission.
Tool execution requires an explicit grant.
failure_modes: Memory access treated as action approval.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A small authority schema and transition table used by chapter examples and future Lean proofs.

proof_targets.target: Every transition preserves or lowers the active authority ceiling unless a governance grant is present.
A missing grant blocks execution rather than becoming default authorization.

chapters.title: Failure Modes of Ungoverned Intelligence
chapters.problem: The book needs an explicit failure model before it can make governance, reliability, or self-improvement claims.
chapters.insufficient: High-level safety language misses earlier engineering failures such as goal misbinding, context pollution, evaluator drift, authority creep, tool overreach, and residual hiding.
chapters.core_claim: Ungoverned intelligence fails through stack-level breakdowns before dramatic catastrophic scenarios are needed.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports stack-level failure analysis through authority laundering, contract drift, evaluator capture, state migration insolvency, dependency drift, bad reliance annotations, recovery failure, and procedural self-ratification.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Does not prove production safety, global alignment, strategic-deception resistance, or actual recovery behavior.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports context and memory failure modes: summaries self-promoting into evidence or authority, hostile quoted text becoming behavioral control, stale/revoked context admission, taint leakage, and unrecorded adequacy gaps.
claim_source_mappings.limits: VCM evidence remains bounded to control-plane properties; no end-to-end model superiority or broad neighboring-system result is claimed.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports execution failure modes around tool calls bypassing contract locks or approvals, secrets entering model context, missing provenance, non-replayable jobs, unverifiable evidence, forgotten failures, and overclaimed security or benchmarks.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Execution-security and benchmark behavior are not reproduced here.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports verification failure modes: failed or timed-out verification treated as success, unsupported claims, contradiction mishandling, protected-axiom self-authorization, and open-domain autoformalization overclaiming.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Does not prove natural-language formalization, theorem validity for arbitrary claims, or whole-system epistemic correctness.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports the governance motivation that power can damage agency, dignity, plurality, consent, and coherence if metaphysical or normative language is not translated into operational boundaries.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Philosophical lineage only; not empirical evidence for consciousness, physics, morality, or AI safety mechanisms.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports early engineering failures such as generated text mistaken for execution, lost artifacts, lost constraints, lost source evidence, forgotten residuals, missing deployment feedback, repeated uncompiled workflows, and claim support inflation.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Architecture proposal only; no deployed failure detector or benchmark/runtime result is proven here.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports resource and simulation failure modes: unstated scope/fidelity/temporal contracts, computability mistaken for feasible execution, ignored memory/heat/bandwidth bottlenecks, and vague simulation arguments used as capability evidence.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Theoretical synthesis only; no physical experiment, simulation benchmark, or independent literature audit was run here.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports long-horizon context-compiler failures where planner hints become access authority, compiled context hides missing evidence or revocation, and source-derived references drift into unsourced third-party citations.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Connector-readable variant; no VCM-Bench, model-facing result, or citation-normalized external-literature support is recorded here.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports runtime failure modes where source-reported benchmark claims are treated as book-verified, unavailable runtime claims are overpromoted, or specialist lanes mutate/escalate authority without control-plane approval and evidence.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Implementation-reference context only until code, logs, release artifacts, or benchmark records are inspected or reproduced.

mechanism: Classify failures by layer.
Map every failure to an invariant and a planned falsification test.
Use failures to motivate boundaries in later chapters.
invariants: Every risk maps to at least one boundary.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A layered failure taxonomy tied to invariants, source queues, and future tests.

proof_targets.target: A component with a failed required invariant cannot be promoted.
An unbounded authority field is detected as a governance failure.

chapters.title: Evidence States and Claim Discipline
chapters.problem: The living book needs a shared language for what kind of claim is being made and what currently supports it.
chapters.insufficient: Without explicit support states, conceptual architecture prose can accidentally read like empirical proof.
chapters.core_claim: Every major claim should carry both a claim label and a support state, and it should move only when source ingestion, prototype inspection, or actual tests justify the transition.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Benchmark lifecycle, ledgers, anti-Goodhart safeguards, wall diagnosis, regression duties, and residual accounting support the rule that benchmark evidence must be scoped before it moves a claim.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Does not prove any benchmark result in this repository or justify treating a benchmark score as permanent intelligence evidence.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Proof/citation/procedure-carrying claim tiers, belief revision, contradiction handling, and downgrade/block behavior support explicit claim labels, support states, and failed-verification demotion.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Does not prove open-domain autoformalization, theorem validity for arbitrary claims, or whole-system epistemic correctness.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Artifact graphs, claim and verification ledgers, residuals, feedback, benchmarks, and the rule of durability support keeping claims, evidence, failures, and regression coverage as durable records.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Does not prove a completed VIEA deployment or any benchmark/runtime result in this repository.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Retrieval-bounded dossiers, proposition states, adversarial review, SME checkpoints, and final sign-off support the distinction between verified, inferred, unsupported, and review-required propositions.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Does not prove the UAT protocol discovers novel truth or that its thresholds are validated here.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Verified Epistemic Units, verification supply chains, contestability, fork, exit, and audit language support structured claim/evidence objects and governance-visible review paths.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Speculative economic or epistemic-liquidity framing is not treated as implemented economics or external corroboration.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: The distinction between context length and verification workspace supports the rule that source ingestion or large context does not itself verify a claim without adequacy, contradiction, and coverage checks.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Does not prove the proposed verification-bandwidth tests or contradiction-rate claims have been run here.

mechanism: Use Appendix C as the claim ledger.
Separate claim label from support state so design rationales, hypotheses, measurements, mechanisms, and speculative claims are not collapsed.
Require source notes before promoting claims to source-derived.
Require evidence bundles, including negative or inconclusive results, before promoting test-backed labels.
invariants: Negative and inconclusive results remain visible.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A claim record schema, claim-label table, support-state transition table, evidence-bundle template, and validation check.

proof_targets.target: Support-state transitions require the corresponding evidence artifact.
A claim cannot be promoted when required evidence is absent.

chapters.title: Human Intent as a Formal Input
chapters.problem: A governed stack must start from human intent without letting natural-language ambiguity become unrestricted execution authority.
chapters.insufficient: Prompt-only workflows blur request, requirements, permission, risk tolerance, and acceptance criteria.
chapters.core_claim: Human intent should enter the stack as a structured contract with goals, constraints, authority, artifacts, evidence requirements, and stop conditions.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports converting human intent into structured command contracts with role, objective, context, constraints, procedure, output contract, verification, failure behavior, artifacts, routing, runtime execution, feedback, residuals, and regressions.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Does not prove automated intent parsing, hidden-authority inference, or deployed intent-to-execution behavior.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports treating instructions as bounded operational language with roles, objectives, context, constraints, procedure, output, verification, failure behavior, handoff artifacts, guards, entry/exit rules, loops, recursion, and recovery paths.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Vocabulary and promptcraft framework only; no empirical prompt-performance result or local test is present.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports compiling natural-language goals into decomposed, dependency-aware, tier-annotated plans with primitive schemas, scheduling, fallback requirements, and failure-driven replanning.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Conceptual design only here; no verified implementation, scheduler run, or benchmark result is present in this project.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports lowering goals, constraints, interfaces, resources, and quality gates into source plans, typed semantic atoms, target instructions, validators, DAGs, and repairable artifacts.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Architecture and evaluation-plan source only; no local compiler, task trace suite, or empirical ablation validates the pipeline.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports the execution boundary by lowering only authorized work into typed jobs with contract locks, adjudication, delivery, feedback, audit, replay, residuals, approval gates, and controlled runtime adapters.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Design source only; does not prove tool-effect enforcement, approval behavior, or benchmark/security claims.

mechanism: Parse intent into command contracts.
Separate desired outcome from allowed means.
Attach acceptance criteria and escalation conditions.
interfaces: Execution consumes only authorized task contracts.
invariants: Ambiguity triggers clarification or bounded defaults.
Stop conditions remain attached to the plan.
chapters.minimal_implementation: An intent-contract schema and example transformations from request to governed task.

proof_targets.target: A compiled intent contract preserves declared constraints and stop conditions.
A contract missing required authority cannot compile to an executable job.

chapters.title: Constitutional Alignment Substrate
chapters.problem: The stack needs a normative substrate that constrains goals, plans, execution, and self-modification.
chapters.insufficient: Reactive refusal policies do not define value continuity, moral uncertainty, agency preservation, anti-domination, or self-modification ethics.
chapters.core_claim: Alignment should function as a constitutional substrate whose commitments are operationalized as constraints on plans and system changes.
mechanism: Translate philosophical commitments into operational predicates where possible.
Keep metaphysical claims labeled as interpretation unless separately supported.
Attach constitutional constraints to planning and replacement gates.
interfaces: Verification checks whether normative claims exceed evidence.
invariants: Dignity and agency constraints remain visible.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A compact constitution with operational predicates, open moral uncertainties, and scenario tests.

proof_targets.target: An admitted plan satisfies every active constitutional predicate.
A self-modification that weakens a protected predicate is rejected.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supplies the alignment lineage for coherence, agency, dignity, corrigibility, value conflict, and consciousness caution as constraints on governance rather than empirical guarantees.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Philosophical and heuristic lineage only; it is not empirical proof of consciousness, moral status, or deployed alignment.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Preserves the deep alignment motivation around coherence, plurality, agency, and dignity while requiring translation into rights, boundaries, corrigibility, evidence, and governance.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Metaphysical source; not technical evidence for physics, consciousness, morality, or safety claims.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Frames recursive agency, contestability, correction, and constitutional systems as governance lineage for operational constraints.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Conceptual philosophy, not an empirical or formal proof source for institutional legitimacy or AI behavior.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Provides computational-metaphysics lineage for axioms, kernels, coherence, misalignment, and ethical law as speculative framing for constitutional predicates.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Speculative framing only; not evidence for physics, consciousness, or governance outcomes.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Adds coherence, contestability, verification supply-chain, and governance-interface framing for translating commitments into reviewable units.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Speculative synthesis; not an implemented economic, governance, or security mechanism.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports protected axioms, proof/citation/procedure-carrying claims, contradiction handling, downgrades, and limits around self-rewriting constitutional changes.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Does not solve open-domain autoformalization, verifier quality, or whole-system epistemic correctness.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Provides the operational constitutional vocabulary: truth/relation/task alignment, least sufficient power, non-domination, consent, reversibility, auditability, tool-risk tiers, and self-authorization limits.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Specification source only; no runtime policy engine, red-team suite, Lean proof of moral correctness, or system-prompt evaluation is claimed.

chapters.title: Agency, Dignity, and Corrigibility
chapters.problem: The book needs a precise account of how human agency and dignity constrain powerful optimization.
chapters.insufficient: Safety framed only as harm avoidance can miss domination, lock-in, coercive dependence, and loss of meaningful user control.
chapters.core_claim: A governed ASI stack should preserve human agency, dignity, corrigibility, and contestability as engineering requirements.
mechanism: Represent agency constraints as limits on delegation and manipulation.
Define corrigibility as preserved update and shutdown pathways.
Use contestability and audit rights as governance mechanisms.
interfaces: Execution requires approvals for irreversible effects.
invariants: Users retain meaningful refusal and review channels.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A rights-and-corrigibility checklist attached to high-impact plans.

proof_targets.target: Protected agency rights remain available after an accepted transition.
A transition that removes a required correction pathway is rejected.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supplies agency, dignity, suffering, moral-threshold, and corrigibility concerns as governance constraints to preserve during optimization.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Does not validate a consciousness index, moral-status detector, or empirical dignity-preservation result.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports contestability, recursive correction, public revision, agency, and limits on AI sovereign authority as governance lineage.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Philosophical and socio-technical framing only; not a tested social process or legal system.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Motivates agency, plurality, and dignity preservation as constraints on powerful AI that must not collapse coherence into uniformity.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Metaphysical lineage only; no operational guarantee of agency preservation.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Provides background vocabulary for coherence, misalignment, agency, and ethical law that the chapter translates into technical rights language.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Speculative/computational metaphysics; not evidence for the runtime checklist or social outcomes.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports fork, exit, audit, contestability, value/accounting, and review-market framing relevant to corrigibility and user contestation.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Does not show the epistemic-liquidity or governance mechanism has been implemented or validated.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports agency preservation, consent, non-domination, reversibility, auditability, least sufficient power, memory governance, and no self-authorization of sovereignty.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Constitutional specification only; does not prove deployed corrigibility, manipulation resistance, or approval-gate behavior.

chapters.title: Moral Uncertainty and Value Conflict
chapters.problem: A self-improving system will face value conflicts that cannot be honestly collapsed into one scalar objective.
chapters.insufficient: Single-objective optimization hides moral uncertainty and encourages premature resolution of contested values.
chapters.core_claim: Value conflicts should be represented as explicit unresolved obligations, review paths, and bounded decisions rather than hidden inside reward functions.
mechanism: Classify conflicts by value type, stakeholder, reversibility, and evidence requirement.
Escalate unresolved conflicts to tribunal or human review.
Record decisions and residual moral uncertainty.
invariants: Speculative moral theory cannot silently authorize action.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A value-conflict record schema and scenario library.

proof_targets.target: A decision with unresolved protected conflicts carries a residual conflict record.
A high-stakes conflict cannot bypass the required review predicate.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Frames agency, contestability, recursive correction, constitutional failure, and objections as reasons to preserve disputed values rather than freeze one authority layer.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Conceptual governance lineage; not a complete moral theory or empirical conflict-resolution result.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supplies the chapter vocabulary for value conflict, suffering, dignity, agency, moral thresholds, coherence, and constraints under uncertainty.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Heuristic and speculative material must remain bounded; not a validated measure of moral status or value correctness.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Adds contestable claim/evidence units, verification supply-chain framing, fork/exit/audit concepts, and coherence/liquidity language for reviewable disagreement.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Speculative synthesis; no implemented review market, economic mechanism, or external governance evidence.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Provides adversarial review, retrieval-bounded verification, proposition states, unsupported-claim removal, SME checkpoints, and termination criteria for contested claims.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Protocol design only; no reproduced benchmark, implemented local pipeline, or independent validation of thresholds.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports explicit support tiers, contradiction detection, belief revision, defeaters, downgrades, and non-assumption that open-domain formalization is solved.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Does not settle moral uncertainty or prove reviewer/verifier correctness.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports runtime checks for stakeholders, uncertainty, consent, reversibility, least sufficient power, truth/relation/task tensions, and non-domination.
claim_source_mappings.limits: No policy engine or red-team result is present; constitutional text cannot by itself resolve value conflicts.

chapters.title: Governance Rights: Fork, Exit, and Audit
chapters.problem: Powerful AI infrastructure needs rights and mechanisms that prevent governance lock-in and hidden control.
chapters.insufficient: Policy statements about transparency are weak if users cannot audit, contest, exit, or fork the system in practice.
chapters.core_claim: Fork, exit, audit, dissent, and contestability should be treated as technical governance interfaces, not only political ideals.
mechanism: Represent rights as capabilities and required interfaces.
Attach audit artifacts to governance decisions.
Treat fork/exit as preserved escape hatches against lock-in.
invariants: Audit records cannot be silently deleted.
Fork rights do not bypass safety constraints.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A governance-rights table with required artifacts, authorities, and failure tests.

proof_targets.target: A governance transition preserves required audit and exit capabilities.
A transition that removes a protected right is rejected or marked invalid.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports contestable recursive governance, public correction, agency, institutional memory, and resistance to frozen sovereign authority.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Not a tested institution, legal guarantee, or empirical governance result.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Provides fork, exit, audit, contestability, verification supply-chain, value/accounting, and governance-interface framing.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Speculative and connector-derived synthesis; no implemented economic or governance mechanism is claimed.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Adds dignity, agency, plurality, corrigibility, and governance constraints that rights interfaces should preserve under power.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Normative lineage only; not empirical evidence that rights are preserved.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports the technical need for hidden credential boundaries, handle-based authority, logs, compartments, and policy injection points around audit and authority use.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Architecture/specification only; no kernel implementation, side-channel validation, or security audit is claimed.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports claim graphs, protected axioms, contradiction handling, downgrades, and blocked self-authorizing changes as review discipline for governance decisions.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Does not prove whole-system epistemic correctness, autoformalization, or governance legitimacy.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports adversarial review, proposition tiering, dossier-bounded verification, unsupported-claim removal, and human sign-off checkpoints for contested governance claims.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Protocol design only; no implemented review process or benchmark result is reproduced.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports auditability, agency preservation, consent, reversibility, least sufficient power, risk-tiered tool use, red-team evaluations, version control, and self-improvement freezes.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Constitutional specification only; not proof of deployed governance rights, fork safety, or audit availability.

chapters.title: Stable Capability Fields
chapters.problem: The stack needs stable semantic boundaries so implementations can improve without changing what a capability means.
chapters.insufficient: Plugin replacement and model swapping do not define identity, authority, qualification evidence, or rollback semantics.
chapters.core_claim: A stable capability field is a governed boundary with identity, interface, authority ceiling, qualification evidence, and rollback policy.
mechanism: Separate field identity from implementation.
Bind qualification claims to exact artifacts.
Represent lifecycle transitions such as shadow, canary, qualified, default, deprecated, and retired.

handoff_or_recovery_notes: 2026-06-24 browser-GPT fast-generation planning note; use as author-intent and literature-queue context only.

chapters.problem: The stack needs to reduce serial token-generation latency without treating raw tokens per second as intelligence or bypassing verification.
chapters.insufficient: Standard autoregressive decoding, raw throughput benchmarks, and isolated serving optimizations do not specify when a faster generation mode is acceptable, how it is verified, or how its failures block promotion.
chapters.core_claim: Fast generation should be treated as a governed, planner-selected choice among autoregressive, speculative, multi-token, diffusion, early-exit, state-space, KV-cache, and hybrid modes selected by task risk, quality target, latency budget, compute, memory, and verification burden.
mechanism: Separate raw generation speed from effective verified tokens per second and useful solution per second.
Let PlanForge select a generation mode from task requirements, risk tier, latency budget, context shape, and verifier availability.
Use draft, verify, repair, fallback, and benchmark records before promoting any accelerated mode.
Route VCM context packets into the selected generator and route accepted outputs through Spinoza, Talos, Benchmaxxing, and SCF gates.
Treat multi-seed diffusion and hybrid AR, MTP, and diffusion systems as research hypotheses until source-specific experiments or stronger evidence transitions exist.
interfaces: VCM supplies the bounded context packet.
The generator emits draft tokens, sketches, spans, or candidates.
Spinoza or a verifier checks acceptance predicates.
Talos turns accepted output into artifacts.
SCF governs whether a mode is promoted, quarantined, or left experimental.
invariants: No fast generation mode is promoted on raw tokens per second alone.
Every accelerated route names its generation mode, verifier, acceptance predicate, risk tier, and fallback.
Accepted output is counted separately from proposed output.
High-risk tasks can require slower verified generation even when a faster mode exists.
Speculative, diffusion, MTP, and hybrid claims remain at argument level until source-specific tests, reproduced results, or accepted external-literature support justify promotion.
failure_modes: Speed hides lower answer quality or higher repair cost.
The verifier becomes the true bottleneck.
Draft heads, diffusion sketches, or cached continuations drift out of distribution.
Memory bandwidth, KV-cache pressure, or parallel sampling cost erases token-speed gains.
Multi-seed generation increases sample count without improving useful solution per second.
Fallback is omitted after rejection or uncertainty.

invariants: Speculative simulation metaphysics stays separated from engineering claims.

The evidence matrix records claim boundaries and support states.

Consumer readiness is not downstream success.
Non-claims survive transfer into later chapters.

Expose alias, freshness, coverage, active-token, work-saving, and loop-exit fields as auditable contract outputs.
Compare slot-only, slot-plus-winding, FIFO, LRU, content-gated, and ordinary attention or memory baselines before promoting performance claims.

Attention and memory modules consume contract fields as diagnostic guardrails.
Benchmark adapters and Theseus transfer lanes decide whether structural facts survive actual workloads.
Proof contracts define the finite boundary of the claim.

Recurrence budgets include exits and guardrails.

parts.title: Part IV - Evidence, Implementation, and the Living Book
parts.part_job: Turn the architecture into an accountable research program: executable specifications, Lean proof envelopes, benchmark ratchets, reference traces, implementation roadmap, living-book method, and bibliography discipline.

chapters.title: Executable Specifications and Lean Proof Envelope
chapters.problem: The book needs to decide which architecture claims should become executable specs or Lean proofs.
chapters.insufficient: Formal methods are ineffective when applied to vague philosophical claims instead of operational predicates and transitions.
chapters.core_claim: Lean proofs and executable specs should target small invariants: authority, support states, SCF replacement, job lifecycle, context adequacy, and residual records.
mechanism: Translate mechanisms into predicates and transition rules.
Keep proof manifest generated from the outline.
Distinguish scaffolded, implemented, blocked, and retired proof targets.
invariants: No theorem is claimed proven without `lake build`.
Broad claims are decomposed before formalization.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A proof manifest, Lean workspace, and first invariant modules for support-state and authority checks.

proof_targets.target: A proof target marked implemented has an existing module and passes the build.
A proof target for a non-operational claim remains planned or blocked, not implemented.

chapters.title: Benchmark Ratchets and Anti-Goodhart Evidence
chapters.problem: The architecture needs a way to move capability claims through evidence without overfitting to fixed benchmarks.
chapters.insufficient: Benchmarks can saturate, be gamed, erase regressions, or fail to represent real requirements.
chapters.core_claim: Benchmark ratchets should preserve regressions, create harder frontiers, record residuals, and resist Goodhart pressure.
mechanism: Track benchmark mastery thresholds and saturation.
Generate regressions and hidden checks.
Move residual failures into escrow and new frontier tasks.
Record negative and inconclusive results as evidence, not cleanup.
interfaces: Routing and SCFs use readiness gates.
invariants: No test success is claimed without execution.
Synthetic and empirical evidence remain distinct.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A benchmark ratchet record with frontier, mastery, residuals, regressions, and anti-Goodhart checks.

proof_targets.target: A capability promotion requires benchmark evidence and preserved regression records.
A saturated benchmark cannot be the sole basis for higher readiness promotion.

chapters.title: Policy Optimization and Learning from Feedback
chapters.problem: A governed stack needs to convert feedback, verification, benchmark pressure, and failure into better future behavior without allowing reward signals to bypass evidence, authority, or rollback boundaries.
chapters.insufficient: RLHF, preference optimization, and verifier rewards are often treated as model-alignment recipes rather than governed update mechanisms across planners, routers, context policies, execution policies, and generation modes.
chapters.core_claim: Policy optimization is the stack's learning actuator: it can update planners, routers, context selectors, verifiers, execution policies, and generators, but governance decides which feedback is admissible and whether any update may be promoted.
mechanism: Classify the target policy: planner, VCM, router, generator, verifier, execution, governance, or generation mode.
Record the feedback source: human preference, verifier reward, benchmark result, tool result, context-grounding check, governance check, or latency/resource signal.
Choose a training/update family by the policy being optimized, the feedback available, and the failure mode that must be blocked.
Constrain the update with drift bounds, evidence gates, rollback plans, and residual accounting.
Evaluate for task success, regressions, reward hacking, authority compliance, and cost before promotion.
interfaces: Benchmarks and verifiers produce reward or preference signals.
VCM and artifact graphs preserve the training/evaluation context and evidence references.
Talos records training, tool, and evaluation artifacts.
Spinoza and UAT audit reward meaning, verifier failures, and reward-hacking probes.
SCF gates decide promotion, quarantine, rollback, or continued experiment status.
invariants: Reward is not evidence unless the verifier and evaluation boundary are recorded.
Policy updates cannot expand authority by training side effect.
A faster or shorter policy is not promoted unless task success and regressions are preserved.
Reward hacking probes are part of the update record, not optional commentary.
Rollback remains available for promoted updates.
failure_modes: Over-optimization for preference style rather than truth or task success.
Policy drift crossing SCF authority or readiness boundaries.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A policy optimization record schema, fixture, and finite Lean predicates that separate feedback, update constraints, evaluation, governance gates, rollback, and non-claims from unrun training results.

proof_targets.target: An admitted policy update records target layer, reward signal, update constraint, evaluation refs, governance gates, and rollback plan.
A policy update with unverified reward or missing governance gate cannot be promoted.

chapters.title: Integrated Reference Architecture
chapters.problem: Readers need to see how the layers operate as one machine from intent to governed action and improvement.
chapters.insufficient: Layer chapters can still feel disconnected unless the book traces the complete control flow and artifacts.
chapters.core_claim: The stack can be specified as an integrated reference architecture with typed handoffs, authority stops, artifacts, evidence updates, and self-improvement gates.
mechanism: Trace user intent through constitution, governance, planning, VCM, routing, verification, execution, evidence, and improvement.
Show the compression loop embedded inside the broader governance, memory, routing, and verification stack.
Show which artifact each layer emits.
Identify where authority can stop or reroute the process.
interfaces: Artifacts and evidence ledgers provide continuity.
invariants: Self-improvement follows evidence and authority gates.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A reference flow diagram, interface table, and end-to-end trace example marked conceptual until implemented.

proof_targets.target: An end-to-end trace contains required artifacts for each layer handoff.
A trace with a missing governance gate cannot be marked valid.

chapters.title: Project Theseus as Report-First Implementation Reference
chapters.problem: The book needs a concrete implementation-reference chapter for how the ASI stack can be operated as report-first local machinery instead of only conceptual layer diagrams.
chapters.insufficient: A prototype roadmap is not enough unless readers can see how goals, plans, arms, reports, gates, residuals, checkpoints, compute, and operator surfaces interact in a working research system.
chapters.core_claim: Project Theseus should be mined as a report-first implementation reference for RMI, showing how pressure, residuals, gates, ledgers, operator work boards, checkpoints, and self-evolution governance can instantiate the ASI stack without promoting local results as general capability claims.
mechanism: Treat reports and ledgers as the contract, with dashboards as surfaces over those artifacts.
Use the loop pressure -> attempt -> residual -> diagnosis -> compression -> verification -> structure -> new pressure as implementation discipline.
Gate heavy training and self-evolution through architecture, preflight, resource, and candidate-promotion checks.
Route operator work through a durable work board, command vocabulary, node registry, hooks, and feedback reports.
Keep sparse teacher use proposal-first and guarded by branches, checks, benchmark regressions, and review.
interfaces: The plan compiler supplies contracts, semantic IR DAGs, VCM slices, routes, claim targets, and replay traces.
Octopus arms, SymLiquid CGS, SparkStream control, and Hive runtime map stack layers into reports and configs.
Genesis-style artifact kernels preserve claims, critiques, benchmark results, tool promotions, architecture decisions, and feedback.
Capability matrices and benchmark ledgers expose readiness and residuals without turning local reports into public empirical proof.
invariants: The frontier moves while the regression floor holds.
Promotion uses external-inference-zero evidence where the local gate requires it.
Reports survive the run and are not replaced by dashboard prose.
Private experiments and public calibration remain separated.
Unknown task kinds are blocked rather than guessed.
Architecture changes follow the intervention ladder.
failure_modes: Remote work proceeds without allowlists, TTLs, or kill switches.
Local prototype results are overclaimed as public evidence.
chapters.minimal_implementation: An implementation-reference crosswalk table from ASI stack layer to Theseus report, config, or tool surface, with evidence state and public claim boundary for each row.

proof_targets.target: An implementation-reference claim names the report, config, or tool surface and does not rely on dashboard prose alone.
A capability or self-evolution promotion is blocked when required gate reports are absent or failing.

chapters.title: Prototype Roadmap
chapters.problem: The architecture needs a build sequence that does not introduce agency or self-improvement before auditability and governance are credible.
chapters.insufficient: A roadmap that jumps to autonomous improvement skips source matrices, artifact graphs, claim ledgers, tests, and authority controls.
chapters.core_claim: The prototype should grow in staged increments from source matrix and artifact graph to governed capability replacement.
mechanism: Start with source inventory, claim ledger, artifact graph, and validation.
Add planner, VCM, typed execution, verification, benchmark ratchets, and SCF gates in order.
Delay recursive self-improvement until evaluator and governance integrity are credible.
interfaces: Each phase unlocks a later layer.
invariants: Roadmap milestones are not test results.
failure_modes: Skipping verification because prototypes feel useful.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A phase table with deliverables, gates, blockers, and acceptance criteria.

proof_targets.target: A roadmap phase can unlock a dependent phase only after acceptance gates pass.
A phase milestone cannot promote a claim without evidence artifacts.

chapters.title: Living Book Methodology
chapters.problem: The book itself must remain a living technical system rather than a static anthology.
chapters.insufficient: Static manuscripts cannot show source additions, claim-state movement, deprecations, proof updates, render status, or test history.
chapters.core_claim: The living book should model the ASI stack discipline through manifest-driven Quarto, source queues, claim/evidence matrices, proof manifests, tests, changelogs, and releases.
mechanism: Use `book_structure.json` for order and dynamic chapters.
Use `docs/book_outline.md` for drafting, source queues, and Lean scope.
Treat conversation-mined packets as author-intent and lineage context, not as independent evidence.
Use drafting annotations such as [SOURCE], [AUTHOR INTENT], [SYNTHESIS], [EXPERIMENT], and [OPEN] in private notes or source notes when helpful.
Require meaningful updates to sync scaffold, proof manifest, validation, render, and changelog.
failure_modes: Public site diverges from Quarto source.
Readers cannot tell which claims are speculative.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A public Quarto repo with dynamic scaffold, source matrix, claim matrix, proof manifest, validation, and GitHub Pages.

proof_targets.target: Every chapter in the manifest has outline proof targets and generated claim placeholders.
A structural update without regenerated scaffold/proof manifest is invalid.

chapters.title: Open Research Agenda and Bibliography Plan
chapters.problem: The book needs a managed research backlog and bibliography plan so new papers can be inserted without destabilizing the architecture.
chapters.insufficient: A pile of sources or ad hoc citations does not tell future writing agents what to load, compare, prove, test, or defer.
chapters.core_claim: The bibliography and research agenda should map source families, external literature queues, missing papers, proof targets, experiment backlogs, and chapter insertion rules.
mechanism: Maintain source inventory, source notes, bibliography, and source-loading queues.

Track recovered and missing items separately: Field of God AI Constitution, Circle Calculus, and Project Theseus/SymLiquid are now local/public project sources; Genesis Engine, Genesis Foundry, BBVCA detail, Spinoza detail, VCM review conflicts, and private empirical results remain recovery or evidence-backlog items.
Use triage rules to update chapters or add precise new ones.
interfaces: Appendix G lists corpus and external literature queue.
Book outline tells future agents what to mine.
invariants: Do not cite unread sources as support.
New papers update inventory before chapter claims.
failure_modes: Duplicate chapter creation for overlapping ideas.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A bibliography plan with external literature areas, source-note backlog, and insertion/merge rules.

proof_targets.target: A source-derived claim requires a source note or equivalent ingested-source artifact.
A new source cannot be assigned to a non-existent chapter id.
